question: What is the nominal volume of the Cold Leg?

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters—such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures—using thermal hydraulic modeling and hand calculations. It details how uncertainties and design considerations are addressed to ensure efficient operation, maximize electrical generation, and maintain safety margins, particularly emphasizing the low pressure losses that support the plant’s natural circulation design.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer, integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and separated by a baffle plate, regulates Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface during various operating conditions. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure, with instrumentation providing real-time data to control systems, and is designed to handle pressure transients without triggering unnecessary reactor trips or safety system actuations.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components within the NuScale reactor coolant system. It specifies the types of steel and alloys used for various parts, such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting, all chosen for their corrosion resistance and compliance with relevant BPVC material specifications. The section concludes with a reference to a source on nuclear energy.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core using natural circulation and interfaces with several other systems for safety and operational functions, including degasification and emergency core cooling. Its pressure-retaining parts help maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, preventing the release of radionuclides.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat during non-LOCA events when normal cooling is unavailable, ensuring safe shutdown of the NuScale Power Module without external power. The system maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours, protecting fuel and pressure boundaries, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs and criteria.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

edges.description: Section A references Section 5.4 for further description of the steam generators, while Section B is content from Section 5.4, thus Section A cross-references Section B for detailed component information.

Section A describes the functional and physical integration of the pressurizer with the reactor vessel, while Section B provides cross-references to other sections for detailed material specifications of pressurizer components, connecting them through the shared component descriptions.

Section B discusses the establishment of steady state values for system parameters, which would include the nominal volume of the Cold Leg described in Section A, thus Section B provides the evaluative context for the design features outlined in Section A.

Section A describes the system-level function and connections of the RCS and pressurizer, while Section B provides specific design details about the pressurizer's integration and flow path with the RCS, with both sections referencing the physical and functional relationship between the pressurizer and the RCS but neither providing the nominal volume of the Cold Leg.

Section A provides a general system description of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) and its components, while Section B specifies detailed material and component information for the pressurizer, which is a subsystem within the RCS described in Section A.

Section A describes the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), while Section B discusses the system evaluation criteria and steady state parameter values, with both sections connected by their focus on the operational parameters and performance of the DHRS and related systems, though neither directly provides the nominal volume of the Cold Leg.

Section A provides a general description of the reactor coolant system's components, which includes the cold leg, while Section B discusses the evaluation of system parameters (such as volumes) necessary for optimal performance, implying that the nominal volume of the cold leg is determined based on the system's design as described in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system (RCS), which includes the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and integral pressurizer, while Section B specifies detailed design information about the pressurizer components that are part of the RCS described in Section A.

Section A describes the function and design basis of the Decay Heat Removal System, which operates within the Reactor Coolant System summarized in Section B, thus Section A provides specific subsystem details for the general system overview in Section B.
